coredata.dc:title: An evaluation of the response of vegetation greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature-based remote sensing indicators to drought stress
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Hao
Xu, Zhenheng
Liu, Hao
coredata.dc:description: Drought seriously threatens the food and ecological security of many countries in the world. Currently, various remote sensing indicators of vegetation status have been utilized in drought monitoring and warning. This study classified them into four types according to the biophysical characteristics of vegetation, i.e., temperature, moisture, fluorescence, and greenness-based indicators. However, their responses to drought stress are still less clear at present, which is not conducive to remote sensing of drought. Hence, a comprehensive evaluation of their responses to drought was conducted in this study. MODIS spectral reflectance and land surface temperature products were used to obtain temperature, moisture, and greenness-based indicators, while five solar-induced chlorophyll fluorescence datasets were used to obtain fluorescence-based indicators. Soil moisture anomaly, Standardized Precipitation Index, and Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index calculated by reanalysis and in-situ meteorological data were employed to indicate drought stress. The Chinese mainland was selected as the study area and the study period covers the year from 2000 to 2020 with months from May to September, the vegetation growing period. Evaluation results demonstrated that: 1) The responses of all indicators to drought stress were the most sensitive in arid areas, the second in transitional areas, and the last in humid areas; Monitoring or evaluation of drought with remote sensing vegetation indicators was more suitable for arid and transitional areas. 2) Among all indicators, the temperature-based indicator was found to be the most sensitive, the moisture-based indicator was in the second, the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators were in the third where the former presented a little more sensitive than the latter. 3) Vegetation temperature and moisture-based indicators should be integrated in the comprehensive indexes of drought rather than only integrate vegetation greenness-based indicators. Soil background effect in the remotely sensed vegetation indicators can be reduced with the feature space model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Remote sensing of drought
Vegetation response
Temperature
Moisture
Greenness
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: A possible solution for removing soil background effect in moisture-based indicators

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation under Grant 6222045; the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 41871338; and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spatial distribution of the study area and meteorological stations with the background of land cover types mapped from the MCD12Q1 in 2020. The geographic coordinate system adopts GCS_WGS_1984. The polygon of climate zones was divided by the Aridity Index. The subfigures (a), (b), and (c) are three relatively small regions in Abaga Banner, Jilin, and Henan, respectively, for Section 5.3.

Spatial distribution of the Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients and their p-values for temporal correlation analysis between remote sensing indicator anomaly and CRA/LAND soil moisture anomaly from May to September 2000 to 2020.

Box plots of the Spearman correlation coefficients (Spearman-r) between remote sensing indicator anomalies and soil moisture anomaly, where (a) and (b) are the statistics with CRA/LAND soil moisture data, (c) and (d) are the statistics with CCI soil moisture data. (a) and (c) do not contain farmland areas, while (b) and (d) contain farmland areas.

Box plots of the Spearman correlation coefficients (Spearman-r) between SIF anomaly from five SIF products and soil moisture anomaly, where (a) and (b) are the statistics with CRA/LAND soil moisture data, (c) and (d) are the statistics with CCI soil moisture data. (a) and (c) do not contain farmland areas, while (b) and (d) contain farmland areas.

Heat map of the mean Spearman correlation coefficient of remote sensing indicator anomalies with CRA/LAND soil moisture anomaly on each vegetation growing month (5, 6, 7, 8, 9) and their average (AVG) in the (a) arid, (b) transitional, and (c) humid climate.

Box plots for the correlation analysis between remote sensing indicator anomalies and SPEI3. (a) and (b) are the statistics based on CRA/LAND meteorological data. (c) and (d) are the statistics based on in-situ meteorological data. (a) and (c) do not contain farmland areas, while (b) and (d) contain farmland areas.

Heat map of the mean Spearman correlation coefficient of remote sensing indicator anomalies with SPEI3 based on CRA/LAND meteorological data on each vegetation growing month (5, 6, 7, 8, 9) and their average (AVG) in the (a) arid, (b) transitional, and (c) humid climate regions.

Box plots for the correlation analysis between remote sensing indicator anomalies and SPI3. (a) and (b) are the statistics based on CRA/LAND meteorological data. (c) and (d) are the statistics based on in-situ meteorological data. (a) and (c) do not contain farmland areas, while (b) and (d) contain farmland areas.

Heat map of the mean Spearman correlation coefficient of remote sensing indicator anomalies with SPI3 based on CRA/LAND meteorological data on each vegetation growing month (5, 6, 7, 8, 9) and their average (AVG) in the (a) arid, (b) transitional, and (c) humid climate regions.
A general form of vegetation response processes with increasing drought stress reproduced (Choat et al., 2018).
The VSDI and NDVI feature space (VSDI/NDVI space model).
The VSDI/NDVI feature space and its fitted dry and wet edges in the research areas of (a) Inner Mongolia, (b) Jilin, and (c) Henan.

Statistical analysis of the Relative Change from RVSDI to RMVSDI on each study month where RVSDI is the Pearson correlation coefficient between VSDI anomaly and SMC soil moisture anomaly and RMVSDI is the Pearson correlation coefficient between MVSDI anomaly and SMC soil moisture anomaly. The right axis NDVI is the average NDVI during multiple years. (a) is in Anyang and Hebi, Henan. (b) is in Nong’an County, Jilin. (c) is in Abaga Banner, Inner Mongolia.

Data sources used in this study and their spatiotemporal resolutions. ESA CCI: European Space Agency Climate Change Initiative, CRA/LAND: China Global Land Reanalysis data, SMC dataset: Soil Moisture in China dataset (2002–2018).

Typical remote sensing indicators of vegetation greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature were used in this study, where P represents a positive correlation and N represents a negative correlation. NDVI: Normalized Difference Vegetation Index, EVI: Enhanced Vegetation Index, NDIIB6: Normalized Difference Infrared Index using Band 6, WET: Tasseled Cap Wetness, VSDI: Visible and Shortwave infrared Drought Index, SIF: sunlight-induced chlorophyll fluorescence, LST: Land Surface Temperature.

row.entry: GOSIF, LT_SIFc, SIF_LUE_JJ, SIF_LUE_PK, and SIF005 Products

head.ce:title: An evaluation of the response of vegetation greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature-based remote sensing indicators to drought stress

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Geoscience and Surveying Engineering, China University of Mining and Technology-Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Geoscience and Surveying Engineering
China University of Mining and Technology-Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Geoscience and Surveying Engineering, China University of Mining and Technology-Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Drought seriously threatens the food and ecological security of many countries in the world. Currently, various remote sensing indicators of vegetation status have been utilized in drought monitoring and warning. This study classified them into four types according to the biophysical characteristics of vegetation, i.e., temperature, moisture, fluorescence, and greenness-based indicators. However, their responses to drought stress are still less clear at present, which is not conducive to remote sensing of drought. Hence, a comprehensive evaluation of their responses to drought was conducted in this study. MODIS spectral reflectance and land surface temperature products were used to obtain temperature, moisture, and greenness-based indicators, while five solar-induced chlorophyll fluorescence datasets were used to obtain fluorescence-based indicators. Soil moisture anomaly, Standardized Precipitation Index, and Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index calculated by reanalysis and in-situ meteorological data were employed to indicate drought stress. The Chinese mainland was selected as the study area and the study period covers the year from 2000 to 2020 with months from May to September, the vegetation growing period. Evaluation results demonstrated that: 1) The responses of all indicators to drought stress were the most sensitive in arid areas, the second in transitional areas, and the last in humid areas; Monitoring or evaluation of drought with remote sensing vegetation indicators was more suitable for arid and transitional areas. 2) Among all indicators, the temperature-based indicator was found to be the most sensitive, the moisture-based indicator was in the second, the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators were in the third where the former presented a little more sensitive than the latter. 3) Vegetation temperature and moisture-based indicators should be integrated in the comprehensive indexes of drought rather than only integrate vegetation greenness-based indicators. Soil background effect in the remotely sensed vegetation indicators can be reduced with the feature space model.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is a necessity for people's livelihoods and economic productivity. When the water supply drops below the minimum required, a drought occurs. Drought generally manifests itself in a natural, frequent, and temporary form. However, its consequences are often catastrophic. According to WMO (World Meteorological Organization) Atlas of Mortality and Economic Losses from Weather, Climate and Water Extremes (1970–2019), droughts rank first in the top 10 disasters that led to the largest human losses with 650 000 deaths during the period. What’s more, droughts are expected to increase in frequency and intensity in many areas as global warming, such as in Europe (Spinoni et al., 2018), China (Yu et al., 2014), western United States (Zhang et al., 2021a), eastern Africa (Williams and Funk, 2011), etc. To prevent and mitigate the adverse effects of drought, various techniques were developed to monitor and predict drought. Among them, remote sensing acts as a very promising technology (AghaKouchak et al., 2015; Su et al., 2017), since its unique advantages such as large-scale continuous observation in space, time-ordered interval observation, multi-spectral observation, etc.

There are various types of droughts such as meteorological drought, agricultural drought, hydrological drought, ecological drought, etc. (Crausbay et al., 2020). Correspondingly, various remotely sensed hydrometeorological variables or land surface parameters were employed in monitoring and evaluating the various droughts. Agricultural and ecological drought are the focus of this study since they pose a direct threat to food and ecology security. Vegetation is an important part of the land surface ecosystem, and its responses to drought are concerns of the agricultural drought and ecological drought. Resultantly, vegetation status has become an important monitoring object. In other words, the variations in vegetation status have been taken as a significant signal in agricultural and ecological drought monitoring, evaluation, and prediction (AghaKouchak et al., 2015; West et al., 2019).

Currently, the remote sensing indicators of vegetation status used in drought monitoring can be roughly divided into four categories: greenness anomaly indicators, moisture anomaly indicators, fluorescence anomaly indicators, and temperature anomaly indicators:

(1) Greenness Anomaly Indicators. The basic assumption of the greenness anomaly indicators is that when the vegetation is under drought stress, the chlorophyll content decreases, and the greenness of the vegetation decreases. Therefore, drought monitoring is carried out by measuring the abnormal degree of remotely sensed vegetation greenness. Most remote sensing indicators of vegetation greenness are based on the difference in vegetation spectral reflectance between the red band and the near-infrared band. Some typical indices include the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), Soil Adjusted Vegetation Index (SAVI), Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI), etc. In addition, there are also some derivative indicators, such as the Leaf Area Index (LAI), Fraction of Photosynthetically Active Radiation(FPAR) (Peng et al., 2019), Percent of Average Seasonal Greenness (PASG), and Start of Season Anomaly (SOSA) (Brown et al., 2008). Some classical greenness anomaly indicators are Standardized Vegetation Index (SVI) (Peters et al., 2002) and Vegetation Condition Index(VCI) (Sun et al., 2013), etc. They have been one of the most widely used indicators in remote sensing of drought (West et al., 2019).

(2) Moisture Anomaly Indicators. The basic assumption of the moisture anomaly indicators is that when vegetation is under drought stress, the water content of its leaves or other tissues decreases. Therefore, drought monitoring is carried out by measuring the anomaly of the remote sensing indicators of vegetation water content. At present, most remote sensing indicators of vegetation moisture are based on the difference in vegetation spectral reflectance among the red, near-infrared, and short-wave infrared bands (Sun et al., 2021). These typical indices include Normalized Difference Infrared Index using Band 6 (NDIIB6) (Sun et al., 2017), Tasseled Cap Wetness (WET) (Marcos et al., 2019), and Visible and Shortwave infrared Drought Index (VSDI) (Zhang et al., 2013), etc. Based on the long-term NDWI data, Water Condition Index (WCI) was constructed to measure drought referring to the VCI (Sun et al., 2013).

(3) Fluorescence Anomaly Indicators. The basic principle of the fluorescence anomaly indicators is that when plants are under drought stress, their photosynthesis decreases and the fluorescence quantum yield decreases. Fluorescence refers to a cold luminescence phenomenon of photoluminescence. For plants, leaves absorb radiant energy in red and blue bands and emit chlorophyll fluorescence in red (around 690 nm) and infrared bands (735 ∼ 780 nm). According to the different excitation light sources, it is divided into active-induced chlorophyll fluorescence and sunlight-induced chlorophyll fluorescence (SIF). The influencing factors of chlorophyll fluorescence are complex involving chlorophyll concentration, leaf structure, canopy structure, solar zenith angle, air temperature, saturated water vapor pressure difference, water stress degree, etc. However, many studies indicated that drought stress can cause an abnormal reduction of SIF. For example, Yoshida et al. (2015) found a drought-related decline in SIF during the 2010 Russian drought event. Geng et al. (2022) demonstrated a SIF decline of around 15% to 19% during an extreme drought event from February to July 2004 in the Wei River Basin, China. Mohammadi et al. (2022) also found unusually fast decreases in SIF during two well-known flash drought events in the United States in 2012 and 2017. Moreover, SIF has been found to be linearly correlated with vegetation photosynthesis based on tower-level observations and process-based modeling (He et al., 2020). Therefore, drought monitoring can be carried out by measuring the abnormal degree of SIF, that is constructing the fluorescence anomaly indicators. A representative of the fluorescence anomaly indicators is the Fluorescence Condition Index (FCI) (Zhang et al., 2021b).

(4) Temperature Anomaly Indicators. The basic assumption of the temperature anomaly indicators is that when a plant suffers drought stress, its transpiration decreases due to the reduction of stomatal opening or the plugging of xylem vessels, which eventually leads to an abnormal increase in canopy temperature and surface temperature. Therefore, the temperature anomaly indicators can be carried out by measuring the anomaly of daytime land surface temperature (LST) or the day-night difference of LST. For example, Kogan (1995) proposed the Temperature Condition Index (TCI) and Sun et al. (2013) proposed the day-night Temperature difference Condition Index (ΔTCI).

In summary, when vegetation suffers drought stress, its features of greenness, water content or moisture, and fluorescence would decline, while their feature of temperature would increase. These vegetation features’ variation forms the basis for remote sensing of agricultural or ecological drought. However, responses of these vegetation greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature-based remote sensing indicators to drought are still less clear. Some studies compared the greenness and moisture-based remote sensing indicators and found that the moisture-based indicators are more sensitive to drought than the greenness-based indicators (Caccamo et al., 2011; Gu et al., 2007). But a different result was found in other research (Bajgiran et al., 2009). Sun et al. (2017) argued that fractional vegetation coverage plays an important role in the above contradiction. They confirmed that moisture-based indicators are more sensitive than greenness-based indicators for vegetated areas. However, this relationship can be reversed by the soil background effects for sparse vegetation areas (Sun et al., 2017). In addition, the fluorescence and greenness-based indicators were also evaluated. It was found that they both have significant reduction under severe drought stress, while the reduction of fluorescence-based indicators is more significant than the greenness-based indicators under moderate drought stress (Lee et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2021; Yoshida et al., 2015). However, there are few studies to carry out the comprehensive comparative analysis of the response process of the above four signals to drought stress, resulting in the change law of vegetation conditions to drought stress that has not yet been fully understood. This also indirectly causes most of the current comprehensive remote sensing indicators of drought to only consider the greenness anomaly indicators, and ignore the moisture, fluorescence, and temperature anomaly indicators, such as Vegetation Drought Response Index (VegDRI) (Brown et al., 2008), Quick Drought Response Index (QuickDRI), and Integrated Surface Drought Index (ISDI) (Wu et al., 2013). Consequently, this study aims to explore the responses of vegetation greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature-based remote sensing indicators to drought stress. Such research is conducive to understanding the process and mechanism of drought-induced anomalies of vegetation conditions. It also contributes to improving the current comprehensive monitoring models of drought.

Fig. 1 illustrates the spatial distribution of the study area and the available meteorological stations located in the study area. The background in Fig. 1 was filled with land cover types obtained from the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) product MCD12Q1 in 2020. In addition to the Chinese mainland as the main study area, three small areas were also selected for specific comparative analysis for Section 5.3. They are Abaga Banner in Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (Fig. 1-a), Nong’an County in Jilin Province (Fig. 1-b), and Anyang and Hebi City in Henan Province (Fig. 1-c). 98% of the land in Abaga Banner is covered by grassland. Nong’an County is an agricultural county where agricultural land accounts for 80.41% of the total area. Anyang City and Hebi City are also mainly covered by cropland with a proportion of around 91.2%.

The MODIS surface reflectance products (MOD09A1) and surface temperature products (MOD11A2) were employed in this study to calculate greenness, moisture, and temperature-based remote sensing indicators. MOD09A1 product has a spatial resolution of 500 m and a temporal resolution of 8-day. There are 7 bands of surface reflectance, which are the red band (620–670 nm), the near-infrared band (841–875 nm), the blue band (453–479 nm), the green band (545–565 nm), SWIR 1 band (1230–1250 nm), SWIR 2 band (1628–1652 nm) and SWIR 3 band (2105–2155 nm). The MOD11A2 provides an average 8-day per-pixel Land Surface Temperature and Emissivity (LST&E) with a spatial resolution of 1 km (km). The period of collected data was from 2000 to 2020 and the study month was from May to September which is the vegetation growing season.

To calculate the fluorescence-based indicator, five global 0.05-degree products of solar-induced chlorophyll fluorescence (SIF) were used in this study, which includes GOSIF (Li and Xiao, 2019), LT_SIFc (Wang et al., 2022), SIF_LUE_JJ & SIF_LUE_PK (Duveiller et al., 2020), and SIF005 (Wen et al., 2020). The temporal resolution, spatial resolution, and period of these dataset are listed in Table 1 . GOSIF is a SIF product created by discrete OCO-2 SIF observations, MODIS data, and meteorological reanalysis data with the Cubist regression tree model. LT_SIFc is a temporally corrected satellite SIF product that combined SIF data from the Global Ozone Monitoring Experiment (GOME), SCanning Imaging Absorption spectroMeter for Atmospheric CHartographY (SCIAMACHY) and GOME-2. SIF_LUE_JJ & SIF_LUE_PK are spatially downscaled products from GOME-2 SIF observations. SIF005 is a downscaled and harmonized SIF product based on the SCIAMACHY and GOME-2 observations. All of these SIF datasets have been validated with ground-based gross primary production (GPP) observations, airborne SIF observations, or satellite SIF observations. For example, it is reported that the SIF estimates from GOSIF were highly correlated with GPP measured at 91 FLUXNET sites (R2 = 0.73, p less than 0.001). The SIF from LT_SIFc was reported that it has well consistency with TROPOMI SIF with R2 of 0.76 and RMSE of 0.11 mWm−2 nm−1 sr−1. Since GOSIF has consistent time coverage with the MOD09A1 and the MOD11A2, it was selected for the comparisons with greenness, moisture, and temperature-based remote sensing indicators from 2000 to 2020. The five SIF products have consistent time coverage from 2007 to 2018. Thus, the five SIF products would be compared to each other from 2007 to 2018.

In addition, the European Space Agency (ESA) Climate Change Initiative (CCI) Remote Sensing Soil Moisture Dataset was also used in this study to calculate soil moisture anomaly as an indicator of drought stress. This dataset contains a long-term series of active, passive, and combined datasets. Passive datasets from 2000 to 2022 with a resolution of 0.25° were used in this study. For ease of calculation, we integrated daily soil moisture into monthly soil moisture using averaging method.

A reanalysis dataset called China Global Land Reanalysis (CRA/LAND) was used for obtaining Soil moisture, Precipitation, and Potential evapotranspiration to measure drought stress. The download address is the National Meteorological Science Data Center (https://data.cma.cn/CRA), the downloaded period is from 2000 to 2020, with a temporal resolution of 1 month and a spatial resolution of 0.25°.

Additionally, a fine spatial resolution soil moisture dataset i.e., SMC dataset: Soil Moisture in China dataset (2002–2018) was used for the comparative analysis over small-scale regions i.e., the regions (a)∼(c) in Fig. 1. The download address is the National Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Scientific Data Center (https://data.tpdc.ac.cn/). The dataset contains terrestrial soil moisture data in China from 2002 to 2018, the unit is m3/m3, the temporal resolution is monthly, and the spatial resolution is 0.05°. It was reported that the SMC dataset is consistent with field measurements (R greater than 0.78) with low RMSE (ubRMSE less than 0.05 m3/m3) (Meng et al., 2021).

As shown in Fig. 1, there are around 300 meteorological stations in the study area. Long-term meteorological data including precipitation and air temperature at these stations were collected in this study from the NCDC (National Climatic Data Center). The downloading address is ftp://ftp.ncdc.noaa.gov/pub/data/noaa/isd-lite/. Table 1 presents the data sources used in this study and their specific spatial and temporal resolutions.

MOD09A1, MOD11A2, and GOSIF products are 8-day composite data. During the data processing, the monthly data was obtained by averaging the data within a month. Moreover, the data with the higher spatial resolution was aggregated into a coarser spatial resolution to keep the spatial resolution consistent. Besides, MODIS land cover type data was used to remove water, urban, snow and ice, desert, and other non-vegetated areas. Moreover, the areas with a vegetation index NDVI of less than 0.3 were also removed because of that we focus on the response of vegetation conditions to drought stress. Additionally, cloud contamination of remote sensing data was circumvented by filtering remote sensing data with their quality assurance (QA) information i.e., surface reflectance state flags in MOD09A1 and quality control layers in MOD11A2. In this study, the surface reflectance data with a cloud state of ‘clear’ in MOD09A1 were retained. Likewise, the land surface temperature data with QA flags of ‘LST produced, good quality, not necessary to examine more detailed QA’ were retained. The surface reflectance and temperature data with other QA flags were excluded in the following evaluations.

In this study, NDVI and EVI were selected to indicate vegetation greenness, which was calculated from the MOD09A1. NDIIB6, WET, and VSDI were selected to indicate vegetation moisture, which was also calculated from the MOD09A1. LST over vegetation areas was selected to indicate vegetation temperature, which was obtained from the MOD11A2. SIF was selected to indicate vegetation fluorescence, which was obtained from the five products GOSIF, LT_SIFc, SIF_LUE_JJ, SIF_LUE_PK, and SIF005. Table 2 lists the specific equations for calculating the above vegetation greenness and moisture indicators, while LST and SIF were obtained from remote sensing products. Among them, the temperature indicator increases with the aggravation of drought stress, while the rest indicators decrease with the aggravation of drought stress. For convenience, the indicator of LST was labeled as a Negative correlation (N), and the other indicators were labeled as a Positive correlation (P) as compared with wetness.

Based on the long-term remote sensing indicators, we can calculate their anomaly indicator in terms of time with the following equation:
(1)
Z
i , j
=
x
i , j
-
μ i
δ i

where
Z
i , j
represents the anomaly indicator in month
i
and year
j
;
x
i , j
is the value of the remote sensing indicator in month
i
and year
j
;
μ i
is the multi-year average value of
x
i , j
;
δ i
is the multi-year standard deviation value of
x
i , j
. It is worth noting that the above equation should be utilized for the indicators that have positive correlation with wetness. As to the indicators that have negative correlation with wetness, the negative value of the
Z
i , j
was used as the anomaly index. Resultantly, when
Z
i , j
is less than 0, the drought occurs. Moreover, the smaller the
Z
i , j
, the more severe the drought stress to vegetation, and vice versa. Hereafter, these anomaly indexes were employed to measure the response of vegetation greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature under drought stress.

Three typical drought indicators are employed here for measuring the drought stress: the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI), the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI), and the soil moisture anomaly. SPI and SPEI can be calculated on different time scales, which is crucial for monitoring droughts with different available water resources. This work mainly studies vegetation responses to drought stress, therefore 3-month SPI and SPEI were calculated. As to the soil moisture anomaly, the Z-score standardization was used again like the equation (1).

Temporal correlation analysis between remote sensing anomaly indicators and drought stress indicators was carried out to evaluate the response of vegetation to drought stress. Specifically, the temporal correlation analysis was conducted at a given pixel using time series data pairs in each month or several months during multiple years. For example, the temporal correlation analysis can be conducted in five months from May to September, the vegetation growing season of the study area, from 2000 to 2020. For this case, the maximum number of data pairs is 5 × 21 = 105. If the temporal correlation analysis was conducted in each month from 2000 to 2020, the maximum number of data pairs is 21. Due to the contamination of the cloud, there may be months when available data pairs are not available. In this study, a minimum of 12 data pairs was adopted to conduct the temporal correlation analysis to ensure that the degree of freedom is greater than 10. The main evaluation metrics of the temporal correlation analysis include Pearson’s correlation coefficient, Spearman rank correlation coefficient, and significance level analysis. Pearson’s correlation coefficient focuses on the linear relationship between variables, and the Spearman rank correlation coefficient focuses on the monotonicity of two sequences. The significance level was measured with the p-value. Normally, the smaller the p-value, the more significant the temporal correlation analysis result. The greater the Pearson or Spearman correlation coefficient, the more sensitive response of remote sensing vegetation indicators to drought stress and vice versa.

To do statistics on the results of temporal correlation analysis, the Chinese Mainland was divided into different major climate regions with an aridity index (AI). The AI is a simple but convenient numerical indicator of climate dryness. It is usually calculated as the ratio of multi-year average precipitation (P) and potential evapotranspiration (PET). In this study, we used the meteorological reanalysis data of CRA_LAND to calculate the AI, which covers 30 years from 1991 to 2020:
(2)
AI =
∑
i = 1
30
(
P i
PET i
)
30

As shown in Fig. 1, the areas with AI less than 0.05 are usually considered Hyper-arid climate regions which were excluded in the following evaluations, because these areas are generally desert regions with almost no vegetation. The areas with AI within 0.05–0.2 are labeled as an arid climate, and that with AI of 0.2–0.5 and 0.5–0.65 are called semi-arid climate and dry subhumid climate, respectively. The rest areas with AI greater than 0.65 are labeled as humid climates. Due to the characteristics of this study area, we merged the semi-arid climate and the dry subhumid climate into one climate zone named the transitional climate zone whose AI is between 0.2 and 0.65.

Fig. 2 presents the results of the temporal correlation analysis between soil moisture anomaly and various vegetation indicators’ anomalies. The correlation analysis was conducted in the vegetation growing period i.e., five months from May to September during 2000–2020. The CRA/LAND soil moisture dataset was employed in this temporal correlation analysis. Results indicate that 1) significant correlations with a p-value less than 0.05 were found for arid and transitional areas, whereas weak significant correlations with a p-value greater than 0.05 and less than 0.3 were found for humid areas. The hyper-arid areas were excluded from the correlation analysis since there is very limited vegetation coverage over these areas. 2) Over arid and transitional areas, there are strong positive correlations between SIF, NDVI, EVI, NDIIB6, WET, and VSDI anomalies and soil moisture anomaly, and there are strong negative correlations between LST anomaly and soil moisture anomaly. The results demonstrated that the drought stress would significantly reduce the vegetation’s greenness, moisture, and fluorescence, while significantly increasing the vegetation temperature. All of the greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature are essential indicators for remote sensing of vegetation drought. Certainly, we can see that there are certain differences among the responses of various remote sensing indicators to drought stress.

To see the differences among the responses of various remote sensing indicators to drought stress, Fig. 3 presents box statistics of Spearman correlation coefficients on different climate-type areas using data pairs from May to September 2000–2020. Both the CRA/LAND soil moisture dataset and the ESA CCI soil moisture dataset were used in the statistics of Fig. 3. Moreover, although water, urban, snow and ice, desert, and other non-vegetated areas were excluded in the investigation by MODIS land cover type data, we are hesitant about farmland because sometimes there is vegetation and sometimes there is no vegetation on farmland. Therefore, the farmland was excluded in Fig. 3 (a) and (c), while it was included in Fig. 3 (b) and (d).

The results in Fig. 3 demonstrate that the evaluation results with the two soil moisture datasets (CRA/LAND and ESA CCI) are very similar. Moreover, the evaluation results with farmland areas are similar to the results without farmland areas. Overall, the correlation coefficients were found to be higher in arid areas, followed by that in transitional areas, and the relatively lowest in humid areas. As to the specific remote sensing indicators, a significant rule can be found for all areas that the temperature-based indicator presented the most sensitive responses. The moisture-based indicators presented the second sensitive response, particularly compared with ESA CCI soil moisture anomaly. Fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators presented the last sensitive responses.

To explore the influence of different SIF products, all five SIF products were used to conduct the temporal correlation analysis in the Chinese mainland. Fig. 4 presents the box statistics of Spearman correlation coefficients on different climate-type areas using data pairs from May to September 2007–2018. Likewise, both the CRA/LAND soil moisture dataset and the ESA CCI soil moisture dataset were used in the statistics. As compared with SIF_LUE_JJ, SIF_LUE_PK, SIF005, and LT_SIFc, the GOSIF did not show relatively poor performance. Instead, the GOSIF presented a higher correlation coefficient than the other SIF products as compared with soil moisture anomaly, such as the box plots in Fig. 4 (a) and (b). In Fig. 4 (c) and (d), there are some times that GOSIF presented a relatively poorer correlation than the other SIF products but not significant. In summary, the GOSIF can be used as a representative of the SIF products in evaluating the response of vegetation fluorescence-based remote sensing indicators to drought stress.

Fig. 5 presents the mean Spearman correlation coefficients on different climate-type areas using data pairs for each month during 2000–2020. The farmland is included in Fig. 5. Results also illustrated that arid areas have the relatively highest sensitivity followed by the transitional areas and the humid areas are the last. Moreover, it quantitatively demonstrated that the temperature-based indicator has the highest sensitivity among the various remote sensing indexes whether in arid, transitional, or humid areas. It also demonstrated that the moisture-based indicators have a higher sensitivity than the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators whether in arid, transitional, or humid areas. As to the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators, the former presented a comparatively sensitive response to the letter in arid, transitional, or humid areas. For example, the mean correlation coefficients of temperature-based LST, moisture-based VSDI, fluorescence-based GOSIF, and greenness-based EVI are around 0.6, 0.50, 0.43, and 0.41, respectively, in arid areas. That mean correlation coefficients are around 0.50, 0.32, 0.18, and 0.21, respectively, in the transitional area.

In addition to soil moisture anomaly, the SPEI was utilized to represent the drought stress. The precipitation and air temperature data from CRA/LAND and in-situ meteorological stations were used. Fig. 6 presents the box statistics of Spearman correlation coefficients using data pairs from May to September 2000–2020. The evaluation results with farmland areas are like the results without farmland areas, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 3. In addition, Fig. 6 indicates that the vegetation response was found to be the most sensitive in arid areas, the second in transitional areas, and relatively the least sensitive in humid areas, which is like the comparisons with soil moisture anomaly. As to the various remote sensing indicators, we also found that the sensitivity sequence is: temperature-based indicator > moisture-based indicator > fluorescence-based indicator ≈ greenness-based indicator, in most cases, which is also similar to the comparisons with soil moisture anomaly.

Fig. 7 presents the mean Spearman correlation coefficients on different climate-type areas using data pairs on each month from 2000 to 2020. The farmland is included in Fig. 7. Note that only CRA/LAND meteorological data was used in this figure. Fig. 7 illustrated that arid areas have the relatively highest sensitivity followed by the transitional areas and the humid areas. Moreover, it demonstrated that the temperature-based indicator has the highest sensitivity among the various remote sensing indexes in transitional and humid areas. The moisture-based indicators also have a higher sensitivity than the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators whether in arid, transitional, or humid areas. The fluorescence-based indicator presented a comparatively sensitive response to the greenness-based indicators. Certainly, in the comparisons with SPEI3, the temperature-based indicator did not manifest its advantage as significantly as that in comparisons with soil moisture anomaly. This is reasonable because soil moisture is a straighter influencer of vegetation water requirements.

In addition to the comparisons with soil moisture anomaly and SPEI3, we also made a comparison with SPI3. Likewise, the precipitation data from CRA/LAND and in-situ meteorological stations were used. Fig. 8 presents the box statistics of Spearman correlation coefficients using data pairs from May to September 2000–2020, and Fig. 9 presents the mean Spearman correlation coefficients for each month. Likewise, the evaluation results with the meteorological data from CRA/LAND and in-situ meteorological stations are very similar. Moreover, the evaluation results with farmland areas are similar to the results without farmland areas, which is consistent with the results as compared with soil moisture anomaly and SPEI3.

Fig. 8 and Fig. 9 both demonstrated that the vegetation responses to drought stress were the most sensitive in arid areas, the second in transitional areas, and the third in humid areas. As to the specific remote sensing indicators, the temperature-based indicator was the most sensitive whether in arid, transitional, or humid areas. The moisture-based indicators were found to be the second most sensitive. As to the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators, it was not significant to conclude which one is more sensitive. Therefore, we think they have comparative performances while the fluorescence-based indicator presented a little bit better than the greenness-based indicators by synthesizing the above studies.

As we know, vegetation is the direct bearer of drought stress. When vegetation suffers drought stress, it would exhibit various response characteristics. These response characteristics cause changes in the remote sensing signal, which forms the bases for remote sensing of drought. Currently, temperature anomaly, greenness anomaly, moisture anomaly, and fluorescence anomaly are the four main response characteristics of vegetation used in remote sensing of drought. The sensitivity of vegetation greenness anomaly and moisture anomaly to drought stress has been studied in previous research (Sun et al., 2017). Some studies compared the vegetation greenness anomaly indexes with the vegetation fluorescence anomaly indexes (Yoshida et al., 2015). However, very few studies have carried out a comprehensive comparative analysis of the above four response characteristics to drought stress at the ecosystem scale. Such research gap was filled in this study as we evaluated the four response characteristics in the Chinese mainland.

Our results suggest that the vegetation responses to drought stress were the most sensitive in arid areas, the second in transitional areas, and the last in humid areas. We believe that this finding is reasonable because the vegetation growing is water-limited in arid and transitional areas, while it is energy-limited in humid areas. In humid areas, the drought would decrease cloudiness and increase the availability of sunlight (Saleska et al., 2007). In addition, the vegetation can use deep roots and deep zone soil moisture to sustain water availability. Both aspects work together, resulting in the response of vegetation to drought stress in humid areas being less sensitive to that in arid and transitional areas. For example, Doughty et al. (2019) did not find an ideal quick decrease of the forest SIF under drought stress in the humid Amazon rainforest. On the contrary, they found a dry-season increase of the forest SIF. They also demonstrated that “the observed dry-season increases of forest SIF are not strongly affected by sun-sensor geometry” (Doughty et al., 2019). In other words, their conclusions are consistent with us that the response of vegetation to drought stress is relatively insensitive in humid areas as that in arid and transitional areas. This finding implies that the monitoring and evaluation of drought with remote sensing indicators of vegetation status may exist greater uncertainty in humid areas and they are more suitable for arid and transitional areas.

As to the four response characteristics, we found that the temperature-based indicator was the most sensitive to drought stress, the moisture-based indicator was in the second, and the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators were in the third where the former presented a little more sensitivity than the latter. We also believe these findings are reasonable because they are consistent with the study of botany on phases of drought response in plants (Choat et al., 2018). Fig. 10 presents a general form of vegetation response process with increasing drought stress reproduced from a botany study (Choat et al., 2018). This botany study indicated that the decreasing stomatal conductance ranks in the first position of the response of plants during drought. On short time scales, the decreasing of stomatal conductance i.e., the stomatal closure induces loss of canopy evaporative cooling through transpiration and thus brings the canopy temperature to rise abnormally. The stomatal closure would not induce the decrease of vegetation moisture, fluorescence, and greenness as fast as the increase of temperature, because the purpose of plants to close stomata is to maintain the normal growth of plants and there is a certain amount of water and chlorophyll storage in the plants. Resultantly, vegetation canopy temperature anomalies have been thought to have the ability to diagnose early symptoms of drought events (i.e., pre-symptomatic detection), or pre-visual detection of drought stresses before visible drought effects occur (Neinavaz et al., 2021).

Although the temperature-based indicator was found the most sensitive to drought stress, it is well known that remotely sensed LST is affected by a variety of surface and atmospheric conditions. Therefore, the LST is suggested to be processed in the future with some normalization like the Crop Water Stress Indicator, CWSI (Han et al., 2018) to highlight the effects of drought stress on vegetation while circumventing the influences from soil background and other climate or meteorological factors. However, there are two main challenges in calculating CWSI from LST where the first is obtaining vegetation canopy temperature from the remotely sensed LST and the second is determining the canopy temperatures at non-transpiring and full-transpiring conditions. Our recent works on the feature space of LST and FVC (Fractional Vegetation Coverage) and the determination of the dry and wet edges of the LST/FVC space would play a significant role in calculating the CWSI from remotely sensed LST (Sun, 2016; Sun and Gao, 2023; Sun and Zhao, 2022).

In addition to the temperature-based remote sensing indicator, the moisture-based remote sensing indicators were demonstrated more sensitive to drought stress than the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators, which implies that the moisture-based indicators are very good for drought monitoring. However, our previous study has demonstrated that the soil background would bring adverse influences on the moisture-based indicators for drought monitoring (Sun et al., 2017). More efforts are needed for removing the influence of soil background on moisture-based indicators. A possible solution is discussed below.

Thirdly, five SIF products (i.e., GOSIF, SIF_LUE_JJ, SIF_LUE_PK, SIF005, and LT_SIFc) were employed in this study and similar results were found among them. However, it is worth noting that remotely sensed surface reflectance data were involved in the production of these SIF products. Although these SIF datasets have been validated with ground-based, airborne, and satellite SIF observations, there is some information overlap among the above fluorescence, greenness, and moisture-based remote sensing indicators. More independent SIF products with similar or superior spatial–temporal coverage and resolution than the existing SIF products are expected to enhance this study.

At last, we demonstrated the outperformance of temperature-based and moisture-based indicators in remote sensing of drought. They should be integrated into the comprehensive remote sensing indicators of drought to detect the drought timelier and more accurately. However, they have not been efficiently integrated into the current comprehensive indicators. For example, the comprehensive indicators represented by VegDRI and QuickDRI only consider the greenness-based indicators including SVI, Percent of Average Seasonal Greenness (PASG), and Start of Season Anomaly (SOSA) retrieved from long-term series NDVI or EVI (Brown et al., 2008). The VegDRI and QuickDRI ignore temperature and moisture-based indicators. On another hand, the comprehensive indicator represented by ISDI considers the remote sensing surface temperature anomaly. However, the remote sensing surface temperature is a mixture of vegetation canopy temperature and soil and other background component temperatures, which confuses the vegetation canopy temperature anomaly and the soil and other background component temperature abnormalities, and it is thus difficult to accurately reflect the vegetation drought stress (Wu et al., 2013). The future comprehensive remote sensing indicators of drought should process the temperature-based and moisture-based indicators by eliminating the interference from the soil background, and integrating them efficiently with the current widely used fluorescence and greenness-based indicators.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A possible solution for removing soil background effect in moisture-based indicators
ce:section.ce:para: To remove the influence of soil background on moisture-based remote sensing indicators, we suggested employing the feature space model like the LST/FVC space (Sun, 2016). Taking VSDI as an example, we constructed a VSDI/NDVI space where the horizontal axis is NDVI and the vertical axis is VSDI shown as in Fig. 11 . There are also one dry edge and one wet edge in the VSDI/NDVI space, where the former represents a very dry land surface and the letter represents a very wet land surface. The four endmember points are representatives of wet soil, dry soil, wet vegetation, and dry vegetation. The left region of the VSDI/NDVI space is dominated by a low vegetation coverage area, the right region is dominated by a high vegetation coverage area, and the middle region is dominated by a partial vegetation coverage area. Given a point in the VSDI/NDVI space, its vertical distance to the dry edge or wet edge can be employed to indicate surface moisture, which is our solution for removing the soil background effect in moisture-based indicators. The specific equation can be expressed as:

where MVSDI is the suggested solution of removing soil background effect in VSDI; VSDIdry and VSDIwet represent the corresponding points on the dry edge and wet edge of VSDI/NDVI space, whose NDVI is the same as that of the given point. The VSDIdry and VSDIwet can be expressed as:
(4)
VSDI dry
= a × NDVI + b
VSDI wet
=
a ′
× NDVI +
b ′

where
a
and
b
are the slope and intercept of the dry edge;
a ′
and
b ′
are the slope and intercept of the wet edge.

According to the equation (3), the most important problem is obtaining the wet and dry edges of the VSDI/NDVI space. Usually, visual fitting according to expertise can be used. However, it is time-consuming and laborious which is not suitable for applications with a large range and long-term series. Here, we developed an auto-fitting method to obtain the dry and wet edges, whose specific steps are as follows:
Dividing the area whose NDVI is 0.2 ∼ 1.0 into 16 intervals (i is 1 ∼ 16) with a step length of 0.05. The 16 intervals were labeled as
M i
.
Selecting the middle value of NDVI in each
M i
labeled as
M\_NDVI i
.
For each
M i
, divide it into 5 subintervals with a step length of 0.01. The subinterval was labeled as
N j
(
j
is 1 ∼ 5).
For each
N j
, the cumulative frequency histogram of VSDI was statistically analyzed, where the VSDI with a cumulative frequency of 5% was set as the value at the dry edge (
VSDI
dry , j
) and that with a cumulative frequency of 95% was set as the value at the wet edge (
VSDI
wet , j
).
For all
VSDI
dry , j
or
VSDI
wet , j
in each
M i
, calculate their mean (
μ
) and standard deviation (
σ
). Checking each
VSDI
dry , j
or
VSDI
wet , j
whether it is
μ - σ ≤
VSDI
dry , j
o r
VSDI
wet , j
≤ μ + σ
. If the answer is no, exclude this
VSDI
dry , j
or
VSDI
wet , j
and recalculate the
μ
and
σ
. Rechecking the rest
VSDI
dry , j
or
VSDI
wet , j
until all of them are greater than
μ - σ
and less than
μ + σ
. Finally, the mean
VSDI
dry , j
(
M\_VSDI
dry , i
) and the mean
VSDI
wet , j
(
M\_VSDI
wet , i
) were considered as the VSDI on the dry and wet edges for each
M i
.
For all pairs of
M\_NDVI i
and
M\_VSDI
dry , i
, linear regression was conducted to obtain the dry edge. Likewise, the wet edge was obtained using all pairs of
M\_NDVI i
and
M\_VSDI
wet , i
with linear regression as shown in the equation (4).

Taking the three small study areas shown in Fig. 1 as an example, Fig. 12 presents the VSDI/NDVI feature space and its fitted dry and wet edges in those research areas, where Fig. 12 (a) is the Abaga Banner in Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region, Fig. 12 (b) is the Nong’an County in Jilin Province, and Fig. 12 (c) is Anyang and Hebi City in Henan Province. It can be seen that the fitting result of the wet and dry edges is satisfying. The outliers can be effectively excluded in fitting the dry and wet edges. It is noteworthy that the VSDI/NDVI space was created with VSDI and NDVI during all study months and years in the study area. Therefore, the fitted dry and wet edges are applicative to all data in the study period and the calculated MVSDI is comparable during the study period.

To evaluate the MVSDI and VSDI, we first calculated the Pearson correlation coefficient between them and soil moisture anomaly based on the SMC dataset on each month from 2000 to 2020. The calculation results can be labeled as RMVSDI and RVSDI, respectively. Subsequently, the relative change of the above two correlation coefficients can be obtained by the equation of Relative Change = (RMVSDI − RVSDI)/abs(RVSDI), where abs() means taking the absolute value. A greater Relative Change indicates a greater improvement of the MVSDI as compared with the VSDI. Fig. 13 presents the statistics of the Relative Change for each month and the average value for all study months. The results illustrated that the MVSDI outperformed the VSDI statistically, which demonstrated the possibility of the proposed solution for removing the soil background effect in VSDI. For example, the first quartile and the third quartile of the average Relative Change varied between 0 and 1 for Anyang and Hebi in Henan and Abaga Banner in Inner Mongolia. In contrast, the values varied between 2 and 7 for Nong’an County in Jilin. Certainly, there are some uncertainties like the comparisons in August of Fig. 13 (a) and (c). We think the uncertainties may come from the determination of the dry and wet edges of the VSDI/NDVI space especially over the high vegetation coverage area. The average NDVI in August is usually higher than in the other months in Fig. 13. Further studies could pay attention to the determination of the dry and wet edges in theoretical or semi-theoretical calculation way rather than the pure auto-fitting method.

Anyway, satellite remote sensing observes the vegetation status from space satellite platforms. Since the limited instantaneous field of view, the soil background inevitably interferes with the remote sensing of vegetation status. Therefore, the satellite remotely sensed temperature, moisture, greenness, and fluorescence signals are the mixture of signals from vegetation and its soil background. Using such mixed remote sensing signals to reflect vegetation status under drought is therefore existing some uncertainties. To monitor drought in a more timely and accurate manner, we suggested that the above remote sensing indicators should be further processed by eliminating the interference of the soil background. The Feature space model like LST/FVC space and VSDI/NDVI space is a possible solution but requires more research attention.

An evaluation of the response of vegetation greenness, moisture, fluorescence, and temperature-based remote sensing indicators to drought stress was conducted in this study. The evaluation results can be concluded that:

1) The above remote sensing indicators presented the greatest correlation coefficients with soil moisture anomaly, 3-month SPI, and 3-month SPEI in arid climate zone. The correlation was in a secondary position in transitional climate zone and the lowest position in humid climate zone. The result implies that the vegetation responses to drought stress were the most sensitive in arid areas, the second in transitional areas, and the last in humid areas. This finding is reasonable because the vegetation growing in our study area is water-limited in arid and transitional areas, while it is energy-limited in humid areas. Drought monitoring and evaluation with remote sensing indicators of vegetation status may be more suitable for arid and transitional areas, rather than humid areas.

2) Among the four types of remote sensing indicators, the temperature-based indicator was found to have the greatest correlation coefficients with soil moisture anomaly, 3-month SPI, and 3-month SPEI in all climate zones. The moisture-based indicator was found to be in a secondary position. The fluorescence and greenness-based indicators were found to be in the lowest position, where the former was a little better than the latter. This result implies that the temperature-based indicator was the most sensitive to drought stress, the moisture-based indicator was in second, and the fluorescence-based and greenness-based indicators were third. This finding is also reasonable because a study of botany indicated that the decreasing stomatal conductance ranks in the first position of the response of plants during drought followed by plant water storage, cuticular conductance, cavitation, and leaf shedding.

3) We suggest that the above remote sensing indicators should be further processed by eliminating the interference of the soil background since the soil background effect would bring uncertainties in drought monitoring. The feature space model like LST/FVC space is a feasible way to conduct such processing. Moreover, we recommend integrating the temperature-based and moisture-based remote sensing indicators in the current widely used comprehensive indicators of drought to monitor drought timelier and more accurately, rather than only using fluorescence and greenness-based remote sensing indicators.

Hao Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhenheng Xu: Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hao Liu: Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to thank any agency or organization that provided the remote sensing data resources, reanalysis data resources, and in-situ meteorological data resources used in this study.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation under Grant 6222045; the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 41871338; and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.
Not applicable.
In accordance with the copyright transfer or open access rules.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Neinavaz et al., 2021
